records.ety: [OE. desturben, destourben, OF. destorber, desturber, destourber, fr. L. disturbare, disturbatum; dis- + turbare to disturb, trouble, turba disorder, tumult, crowd. See Turbid.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Disturbed (?); p. pr. & vb. n. Disturbing.]

defs: To throw into disorder or confusion; to derange; to interrupt the settled state of; to excite from a state of rest.
quotes: Preparing to disturb With all-cofounding war the realms above.
The bellow's noise disturbed his quiet rest.
The utmost which the discontented colonies could do, was to disturb authority.

defs: To agitate the mind of; to deprive of tranquillity; to disquiet; to render uneasy; as, a person is disturbed by receiving an insult, or his mind is disturbed by envy.
To turn from a regular or designed course.
quotes: And disturb His inmost counsels from their destined aim.
